Title: Long-Term Effectiveness of a Clinician-Assisted Digital Cognitive Behavioral Therapy Intervention for Smoking Cessation: Secondary Outcomes From a Randomized Controlled Trial.
Abstract: INTRODUCTION: This study evaluated the secondary effectiveness outcomes for Quit Genius, a digital clinician-assisted cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) intervention for smoking cessation. [cite: 2] METHODS: Adult smokers (N = 556) were randomly assigned to Quit Genius (n = 277), a digital, clinician-assisted CBT intervention or very brief advice (VBA) to stop smoking, an evidence-based, 30-s intervention designed to facilitate quit attempts, coupled with referral to a cessation service (n = 279). [cite: 3] Participants were offered combination nicotine replacement therapy (patches and gum) tailored to individual nicotine dependence. [cite: 4] Analyses (n = 530), by intention-to-treat, compared Quit Genius and VBA at 4, 26, and 52 weeks post-quit date (QD). [cite: 5] The primary outcome was self-reported 7-day point prevalence abstinence (PPA) at 4 weeks post-QD. [cite: 6] Consecutive 7-day point-prevalence abstinence, defined as abstinent at two or more consecutive timepoints, was examined at weeks 26 and 52 to indicate long-term effectiveness. [cite: 7] Abstinence was verified using a random sample of participants with carbon monoxide breath testing of <5 parts per million (n = 280). [cite: 8] RESULTS: Self-reported consecutive 7-day PPA at weeks 26 and 52 for Quit Genius was 27.2% and 22.6%, respectively, compared with VBA which was 16.6% and 13.2% (RR = 1.70, 95% CI, 1.22-2.37; p = .003, 26 weeks; RR = 1.71, 95% CI, 1.17-2.50; P = .005, 52 weeks). [cite: 9] Biochemically verified abstinence was significantly different at 26- (p = .03) but not 52 weeks (p = .16). [cite: 10] Quit Genius participants were more likely to remain abstinent than those who received VBA (RR = 1.71, 95% CI 1.17-2.50; p = .005). [cite: 11] CONCLUSIONS: This study provides secondary evidence for the long-term effectiveness of Quit Genius in comparison with VBA. [cite: 12] Future trials of digital interventions without clinician support and comparisons with active treatment are needed. [cite: 13] IMPLICATIONS: The long-term effectiveness of clinician-assisted digital smoking cessation interventions has not been well studied. [cite: 14] This study established the long-term effectiveness of an extended CBT-based intervention; [cite: 15] results may inform implementation of scalable approaches to smoking cessation in the health system. [cite: 16]

Title: A randomized controlled trial of a smoking cessation smartphone application with a carbon monoxide checker.
Abstract: Evidence of the long-term efficacy of digital therapies for smoking cessation that include a smartphone application (app) is limited. [cite: 35] In this multi-center randomized controlled trial, we tested the efficacy of a novel digital therapy for smoking cessation: the "CureApp Smoking Cessation (CASC)" system, including a CASC smartphone app, a web-based patient management PC software for primary physicians, and a mobile exhaled carbon monoxide (CO) checker. [cite: 36] A total of 584 participants with nicotine dependence were recruited from October 2017 to January 2018, and allocated 1:1 to the CASC intervention group or the control group. [cite: 37] Both groups received a standard smoking cessation treatment with pharmacotherapy and counseling for 12 weeks. [cite: 38] Meanwhile, the intervention group used the CASC system, and the control group used a control-app without a mobile CO checker, each for 24 weeks. [cite: 39] The primary outcome was the biochemically validated continuous abstinence rate (CAR) from weeks 9 to 24. The main secondary outcome was an extended CAR from weeks 9 to 52. Except for 12 participants who did not download or use the apps, 285 participants were assigned to the intervention group, and 287, to the control. [cite: 40] CAR from weeks 9 to 24 in the intervention group was significantly higher than that in the control group (63.9% vs. 50.5%; odds ratio [OR], 1.73; 95% confidence interval [CI], 1.24 to 2.42; P = 0.001). [cite: 41] The CAR from weeks 9 to 52 was also higher in the intervention group than that in the control group (52.3% vs. 41.5%; OR, 1.55; 95% CI, 1.11 to 2.16; P = 0.010). [cite: 42] No specific adverse events caused by the CASC system were reported. [cite: 43] Augmenting standard face-to-face counseling and pharmacotherapy with a novel smartphone app, the CASC system significantly improved long-term CARs compared to standard treatment and a minimally supportive control app. [cite: 44]

Title: Effect of Technology-Assisted Brief Abstinence Game on Long-term Smoking Cessation in Individuals Not Yet Ready to Quit: A Randomized Clinical Trial.
Abstract: IMPORTANCE: Most trials of behavioral or pharmaceutical interventions for people who smoke are limited to individuals reporting they are ready to quit smoking. [cite: 63] Engaging individuals who initially report they are not yet ready to quit in brief, precessation, skills-building interventions (eg, practice quit attempts or nicotine replacement therapy [NRT] sampling) is challenging. [cite: 64] OBJECTIVE: To test an integrated behavioral plus NRT-sampling intervention using a gamification approach supported by mobile health. [cite: 65] DESIGN, SETTING, AND PARTICIPANTS: A multisite randomized clinical trial with site-level 1-to-1 allocation into 2 conditions was conducted in 4 US health care systems. [cite: 66] A total of 433 individuals who were currently smoking and reported at enrollment that they were not ready to quit smoking were enrolled. [cite: 67] The study was conducted from November 7, 2016, to July 31, 2020. INTERVENTIONS: Take a Break (TAB) was a 3-week game experience and included 5 behavioral components (motivational messaging, challenge quizzes, brief abstinence goal setting, mobile health apps for cravings management, and reward points for participation) integrated with NRT sampling. [cite: 68] TAB draws on social cognitive theory and game mechanics concepts to engage participants in health behavior change. [cite: 69] The comparison included NRT sampling only. MAIN OUTCOMES AND MEASURES: Time to first quit attempt (duration from TAB experience to primary outcome) and carbon monoxide level-verified smoking cessation at 6-month follow-up. [cite: 70] All analyses used an intention-to-treat approach. RESULTS: Of the 433 individuals included in the trial, 223 were women (52%); [cite: 71] mean (SD) age was 54 (13) years. [cite: 72] More than half (53% [112 of 213]) of the TAB participants completed 100% of the daily challenge quizzes in the first week, 73% (145 of 199) of participants who completed the goal-setting call set a brief abstinence goal (most frequently 1-2 days of abstinence from cigarettes), and 75% (159 of 213) of participants used the mobile health apps to manage nicotine cravings. [cite: 73] Time to the first quit attempt was lower for the TAB vs comparison group (hazard ratio, 1.68; 95% CI, 1.09-2.60; P = .02). [cite: 74] At the 6-month follow-up, 18% (28 of 160) of TAB participants and 10% (17 of 171) of the comparison (χ2 test, P = .045) participants obtained carbon monoxide level-verified smoking cessation (accounting for clustering of outcomes by site; odds ratio, 1.92; 95% CI, 1.01-3.68; P = .048). [cite: 75] CONCLUSIONS AND RELEVANCE: The findings of this randomized clinical trial demonstrate that individuals not yet ready to quit smoking could be engaged in a brief abstinence game. [cite: 76] Six months later, the TAB group had nearly double the rate of smoking cessation vs the NRT sampling comparison group. [cite: 77] Integrating a skills-building game experience with brief NRT sampling can enhance long-term cessation among those not yet ready to quit smoking. [cite: 78] TRIAL REGISTRATION: ClinicalTrials.gov Identifier: NCT02973425. [cite: 79]

Title: Craving to Quit: A Randomized Controlled Trial of Smartphone App-Based Mindfulness Training for Smoking Cessation.
Abstract: INTRODUCTION: Mindfulness training may reduce smoking rates and lessen the association between craving and smoking. [cite: 103] This trial tested the efficacy of mindfulness training via smartphone app to reduce smoking. [cite: 104] Experience sampling (ES) was used to measure real-time craving, smoking, and mindfulness. [cite: 105] METHODS: A researcher-blind, parallel randomized controlled trial compared the efficacy of mobile mindfulness training with experience sampling (MMT-ES; Craving to Quit) versus experience sampling only (ES) to (1) increase 1-week point-prevalence abstinence rates at 6 months, and (2) lessen the association between craving and smoking. [cite: 106] A modified intent-to-treat approach was used for treatment starters (MMT-ES n = 143; ES n = 182; 72% female, 81% white, age 41 ± 12 year). [cite: 107] RESULTS: No group difference was found in smoking abstinence at 6 months (overall, 11.1%; MMT-ES, 9.8%; ES, 12.1%; χ2(1) = 0.43, p = .51). [cite: 108] From baseline to 6 months, both groups showed a reduction in cigarettes per day (p < .0001), craving strength (p < .0001) and frequency (p < .0001), and an increase in mindfulness (p < .05). [cite: 109] Using ES data, a craving by group interaction was observed (F(1,3785) = 3.71, p = .05) driven by a stronger positive association between craving and cigarettes per day for ES (t = 4.96, p < .0001) versus MMT-ES (t = 2.03, p = .04). [cite: 110] Within MMT-ES, the relationship between craving and cigarettes per day decreased as treatment completion increased (F(1,104) = 4.44, p = .04). [cite: 111] CONCLUSIONS: Although mindfulness training via smartphone app did not lead to reduced smoking rates compared with control, our findings provide preliminary evidence that mindfulness training via smartphone app may help lessen the association between craving and smoking, an effect that may be meaningful to support quitting in the longer term. [cite: 112] IMPLICATIONS: This is the first reported full-scale randomized controlled trial of any smartphone app for smoking cessation. [cite: 113] Findings provide preliminary evidence that smartphone app-based MMT-ES may lessen the association between craving and smoking. [cite: 114] TRIAL REGISTRATION: Clinicaltrials.gov NCT02134509. [cite: 115]

Title: The Stop-tabac smartphone application for smoking cessation: a randomized controlled trial.
Abstract: AIMS: To test whether the Stop-tabac smartphone application (app) increased smoking cessation rates. [cite: 134] DESIGN: A two-arm, parallel-group, individually randomized, double-blind, controlled trial. [cite: 135] SETTING AND PARTICIPANTS: A total of 5293 daily smokers (Stop-tabac = 2639, control = 2654) enrolled on app stores and on the internet in 2019-20, who lived in France or Switzerland. [cite: 136] INTERVENTION AND COMPARATOR: The Stop-tabac application includes immediate feedback during episodes of craving and withdrawal; [cite: 137] individually tailored counseling messages with notifications sent during 6 months; a discussion forum; fact sheets; [cite: 138] modules on nicotine replacement therapy and e-cigarettes; [cite: 139] and calculators of cigarettes not smoked, money saved and days of life gained since quitting. [cite: 140] The control application included five brief pages and calculators as above. [cite: 141] MEASUREMENTS: Primary outcome: self-reported smoking cessation after 6 months (no puff of tobacco in the past 4 weeks), with non-responders counted as smokers. [cite: 142] SECONDARY OUTCOME: self-reported use of nicotine medications. [cite: 143] FINDINGS: Participants were aged 36 years on average; [cite: 144] 66% were women who smoked 15 cigarettes/day, and 64% screened positive for depression. [cite: 145] Stop-tabac participants used the app over a longer period than control participants (23 versus 11 days, P < 0.001). [cite: 146] Smoking cessation rates after 6 months were 9.9% in the Stop-tabac group versus 10.3% in the control group (odds ratio = 0.96, 95% confidence interval = 0.80-1.45, P = 0.63). [cite: 147] Rates of use of nicotine medications after entry in the study were 38 versus 30% after 6 months (χ(2) = 8.3, P = 0.004) in the Stop-tabac and control groups. [cite: 148] After 6 months, 26% of participants in the Stop-tabac group and 8% in the control group said that the app helped them 'a lot' or 'enormously' to quit smoking (χ(2) = 113, P < 0.001). [cite: 149] CONCLUSIONS: In smokers enrolled on the app stores and the internet, allocation to the Stop-tabac smoking cessation app did not increase smoking cessation rates, but increased rates of use of nicotine medications. [cite: 150]

Title: Outcomes and Device Usage for Fully Automated Internet Interventions Designed for a Smartphone or Personal Computer: The MobileQuit Smoking Cessation Randomized Controlled Trial.
Abstract: BACKGROUND: Many best practice smoking cessation programs use fully automated internet interventions designed for nonmobile personal computers (desktop computers, laptops, and tablets). [cite: 160] A relatively small number of smoking cessation interventions have been designed specifically for mobile devices such as smartphones. [cite: 161] OBJECTIVE: This study examined the efficacy and usage patterns of two internet-based best practices smoking cessation interventions. [cite: 162] METHODS: Overall, 1271 smokers who wanted to quit were randomly assigned to (1) MobileQuit (designed for-and constrained its use to-mobile devices, included text messaging, and embodied tunnel information architecture) or (2) QuitOnline (designed for nonmobile desktop or tablet computers, did not include text messages, and used a flexible hybrid matrix-hierarchical information architecture). [cite: 163] Primary outcomes included self-reported 7-day point-prevalence smoking abstinence at 3- and 6-month follow-up assessments. [cite: 164] Program visits were unobtrusively assessed (frequency, duration, and device used for access). [cite: 165] RESULTS: Significantly more MobileQuit participants than QuitOnline participants reported quitting smoking. [cite: 166] Abstinence rates using intention-to-treat analysis were 20.7% (131/633) vs 11.4% (73/638) at 3 months, 24.6% (156/633) vs 19.3% (123/638) at 6 months, and 15.8% (100/633) vs 8.8% (56/638) for both 3 and 6 months. [cite: 167] Using Complete Cases, MobileQuit's advantage was significant at 3 months (45.6% [131/287] vs 28.4% [73/257]) and the combined 3 and 6 months (40.5% [100/247] vs 25.9% [56/216]) but not at 6 months (43.5% [156/359] vs 34.4% [123/329]). [cite: 168] Participants in both conditions reported their program was usable and helpful. [cite: 169] MobileQuit participants visited their program 5 times more frequently than did QuitOnline participants. [cite: 170] Consistent with the MobileQuit's built-in constraint, 89.46% (8820/9859) of its visits were made on an intended mobile device, whereas 47.72% (691/1448) of visits to QuitOnline used an intended nonmobile device. [cite: 171] Among MobileQuit participants, 76.0% (459/604) used only an intended mobile device, 23.0% (139/604) used both mobile and nonmobile devices, and 0.1% (6/604) used only a nonmobile device. [cite: 172] Among QuitOnline participants, 31.3% (137/438) used only the intended nonmobile devices, 16.7% (73/438) used both mobile and nonmobile devices, and 52.1% (228/438) used only mobile devices (primarily smartphones). [cite: 173] CONCLUSIONS: This study provides evidence for optimizing intervention design for smartphones over a usual care internet approach in which interventions are designed primarily for use on nonmobile devices such as desktop computers, laptops. [cite: 174] or tablets. We propose that future internet interventions should be designed for use on all of the devices (multiple screens) that users prefer. [cite: 175] We forecast that the approach of designing internet interventions for mobile vs nonmobile devices will be replaced by internet interventions that use a single Web app designed to be responsive (adapt to different screen sizes and operating systems), share user data across devices, embody a pervasive information architecture, and complemented by text message notifications. [cite: 176] TRIAL REGISTRATION: ClinicalTrials.gov NCT01952236; [cite: 177] https://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT01952236 (Archived by WebCite at http://www.webcitation.org/6zdSxqbf8). [cite: 178]

Title: A Mobile Health Solution Complementing Psychopharmacology-Supported Smoking Cessation: Randomized Controlled Trial.
Abstract: BACKGROUND: Smoking cessation is a persistent leading public health challenge. [cite: 195] Mobile health (mHealth) solutions are emerging to improve smoking cessation treatments. [cite: 196] Previous approaches have proposed supporting cessation with tailored motivational messages. [cite: 197] Some managed to provide short-term improvements in smoking cessation. [cite: 198] Yet, these approaches were either static in terms of personalization or human-based nonscalable solutions. [cite: 199] Additionally, long-term effects were neither presented nor assessed in combination with existing psychopharmacological therapies. [cite: 200] OBJECTIVE: This study aimed to analyze the long-term efficacy of a mobile app supporting psychopharmacological therapy for smoking cessation and complementarily assess the involved innovative technology. [cite: 201] METHODS: A 12-month, randomized, open-label, parallel-group trial comparing smoking cessation rates was performed at Virgen del Rocío University Hospital in Seville (Spain). [cite: 202] Smokers were randomly allocated to a control group (CG) receiving usual care (psychopharmacological treatment, n=120) or an intervention group (IG) receiving psychopharmacological treatment and using a mobile app providing artificial intelligence-generated and tailored smoking cessation support messages (n=120). [cite: 203] The secondary objectives were to analyze health-related quality of life and monitor healthy lifestyle and physical exercise habits. [cite: 204] Safety was assessed according to the presence of adverse events related to the pharmacological therapy. [cite: 205] Per-protocol and intention-to-treat analyses were performed. Incomplete data and multinomial regression analyses were performed to assess the variables influencing participant cessation probability. [cite: 206] The technical solution was assessed according to the precision of the tailored motivational smoking cessation messages and user engagement. [cite: 207] Cessation and no cessation subgroups were compared using t tests. [cite: 208] A voluntary satisfaction questionnaire was administered at the end of the intervention to all participants who completed the trial. [cite: 209] RESULTS: In the IG, abstinence was 2.75 times higher (adjusted OR 3.45, P=.01) in the per-protocol analysis and 2.15 times higher (adjusted OR 3.13, P=.002) in the intention-to-treat analysis. [cite: 210] Lost data analysis and multinomial logistic models showed different patterns in participants who dropped out. [cite: 211] Regarding safety, 14 of 120 (11.7%) IG participants and 13 of 120 (10.8%) CG participants had 19 and 23 adverse events, respectively (P=.84). [cite: 212] None of the clinical secondary objective measures showed relevant differences between the groups. [cite: 213] The system was able to learn and tailor messages for improved effectiveness in supporting smoking cessation but was unable to reduce the time between a message being sent and opened. [cite: 214] In either case, there was no relevant difference between the cessation and no cessation subgroups. [cite: 215] However, a significant difference was found in system engagement at 6 months (P=.04) but not in all subsequent months. [cite: 216] High system appreciation was reported at the end of the study. [cite: 217] CONCLUSIONS: The proposed mHealth solution complementing psychopharmacological therapy showed greater efficacy for achieving 1-year tobacco abstinence as compared with psychopharmacological therapy alone. [cite: 218] It provides a basis for artificial intelligence-based future approaches. [cite: 219] TRIAL REGISTRATION: ClinicalTrials.gov NCT03553173; https://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT03553173. [cite: 220]

Title: Effect of a Mobile Phone Intervention on Quitting Smoking in a Young Adult Population of Smokers: Randomized Controlled Trial.
Abstract: BACKGROUND: Digital mobile technology presents a promising medium for reaching young adults with smoking cessation interventions because they are the heaviest users of this technology. [cite: 245] OBJECTIVE: The aim of this study was to determine the efficacy of an evidence-informed smartphone app for smoking cessation, Crush the Crave (CTC), on reducing smoking prevalence among young adult smokers in comparison with an evidence-informed self-help guide, On the Road to Quitting (OnRQ). [cite: 246] METHODS: A parallel, double-blind, randomized controlled trial with 2 arms was conducted in Canada to evaluate CTC. [cite: 247] In total, 1599 young adult smokers (aged 19 to 29 years) intending to quit smoking in the next 30 days were recruited online and randomized to receive CTC or the control condition OnRQ for a period of 6 months. [cite: 248] The primary outcome measure was self-reported continuous abstinence at the 6-month follow-up. [cite: 249] RESULTS: Overall follow-up rates were 57.41% (918/1599) and 60.48% (967/1599) at 3 and 6 months, respectively. [cite: 250] Moreover, 45.34% (725/1599) of participants completed baseline, 3-, and 6-month follow-up. [cite: 251] Intention-to-treat analysis (last observation carried forward) showed that continuous abstinence (N=1599) at 6 months was not significantly different at 7.8% (64/820) for CTC versus 9.2% (72/779) for OnRQ (odds ratio; OR 0.83, 95% CI 0.59-1.18). [cite: 252] Similarly, 30-day point prevalence abstinence at 6 months was not significantly different at 14.4% (118/820) and 16.9% (132/779) for CTC and OnRQ, respectively (OR 0.82, 95% CI 0.63-1.08). [cite: 253] However, these rates of abstinence were favorable compared with unassisted 30-day quit rates of 11.5% among young adults. [cite: 254] Secondary measures of quit attempts and the number of cigarettes smoked per day at 6-month follow-up did not reveal any significant differences between groups. [cite: 255] For those who completed the 6-month follow-up, 85.1% (359/422) of young adult smokers downloaded CTC as compared with 81.8% (346/423) of OnRQ, χ(2)(1(N=845))=1.6, P=.23. [cite: 256] Furthermore, OnRQ participants reported significantly higher levels of overall satisfaction (mean 3.3 [SD 1.1] vs mean 2.6 [SD 1.3]; t(644)=6.87, P<.001), perceived helpfulness (mean 5.8 [SD 2.4] vs mean 4.3 [SD 2.6], t(657)=8.0, P<.001), and frequency of use (mean 3.6 [SD 1.2] vs mean 3.2 [SD 1.1], t(683)=5.7, P<.001) compared with CTC participants. [cite: 257] CONCLUSIONS: CTC was feasible for delivering cessation support but was not superior to a self-help guide in helping motivated young adults to quit smoking. [cite: 258] CTC will benefit from further formative research to address satisfaction and usage. [cite: 259] As smartphone apps may not serve as useful alternatives to printed self-help guides, there is a need to conduct further research to understand how digital mobile technology smoking cessation interventions for smoking cessation can be improved. [cite: 260] TRIAL REGISTRATION: ClinicalTrials.gov NCT01983150; [cite: 261] http://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT01983150 (Archived by WebCite at http://www.webcitation.org/6VGyc0W0i). [cite: 262]

Title: Effectiveness of the e-Tabac Info Service application for smoking cessation: a pragmatic randomised controlled trial.
Abstract: OBJECTIVE: To compare the effectiveness of the mobile e-Tabac Info Service (e-TIS) application (app) for helping adult smokers quit smoking with current practices. [cite: 282] DESIGN: Pragmatic randomised controlled trial with a 1-year follow-up (2017-2018). SETTING: France, population-wide level. [cite: 283] PARTICIPANTS: 2806 adult smokers who wished to quit smoking were recruited via the website of the French National Mandatory Health Insurance fund. [cite: 284] Of them, 1400 were randomised to the e-TIS app arm and 1406 were randomised to the current practices arm (control). [cite: 285] INTERVENTION: The app involved personalised interactive contacts that included questionnaires, advice, activities and text messages. [cite: 286] All contacts were individually tailored and based on each smoker's progress.In the control group, recommended practices for quitting smoking were described on a non-interactive website. [cite: 287] PRIMARY AND SECONDARY OUTCOMES MEASURES: The primary outcome was 7-day point prevalence abstinence (PPA) at 6 months. [cite: 288] The secondary outcomes included continuous abstinence rates at 6 and 12 months, minimum 24-hour point abstinence at 3 months, minimum 30-day point abstinence at 12 months and number and duration of quit attempts. [cite: 289] RESULTS: There was no difference between the e-TIS and control arms for the primary outcome (12.6% vs 13.7% for 7-day PPA at 6 months, p=0.3949, intention-to-treat analysis). [cite: 290] However, e-TIS participants with high levels of exposure to the app, which was defined by the completion of at least eight activities or questionnaires, showed higher rates of smoking cessation than the control participants (17.6% vs 12.9% for 7-day PPA at 6 months, p=0.0169, per-protocol analysis). [cite: 291] CONCLUSION: Use of the e-TIS app was not associated with a higher rate of smoking cessation. [cite: 292] However, high level of exposure to the e-TIS app may have been more effective than current practices. [cite: 293] TRIAL REGISTRATION NUMBER: NCT02841683. [cite: 294]
